metadata.title: SATELLITE IMAGERY
metadata.summary: SATELLITE IMAGERY Multi-temporal Landsat satellite imagery constituted the primary data source for this study, the Landsat programme, jointly managed...

turns.content:
SATELLITE IMAGERY
Multi-temporal Landsat satellite imagery constituted the primary data source for this study, the Landsat programme, jointly managed by the United States Geological Survey (USGS) and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), provides a continuous, freely accessible archive of medium-resolution (30 m) multispectral Earth observation data dating back to 1972. For the purposes of this study, four reference years were selected: 2000, 2010, 2020, and 2024 to capture decadal LULC transitions and to provide a near-contemporary assessment of prevailing land cover conditions.

Multi-temporal Landsat satellite imagery constituted the primary data source for this study, the Landsat programme, jointly managed by the United States Geological Survey (USGS) and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), provides a continuous, freely accessible archive of medium-resolution (30 m) multispectral Earth observation data dating back to 1972. For the purposes of this study, four reference years were selected: 2000, 2010, 2020, and 2024 to capture decadal LULC transitions and to provide a near-contemporary assessment of prevailing land cover conditions.

turns.content:
I have completed the grounded academic revision of your methodology section, adapting it from the Landsat framework to Sentinel-2 based on your Google Earth Engine (GEE) snippet. 

The revised section has been saved to your project directory:
[SENTINEL2_METHODOLOGY.md](file:///c:/YERBO/Desktop/Courage/precision%20agriculture/SENTINEL2_METHODOLOGY.md)

### Key Academic Refinements Made:

1. **Constellation Dynamics**: Transitioned the sensor description from Landsat legacy sensors (TM, ETM+, OLI) to the **Sentinel-2 twin polar-orbiting constellation** (Sentinel-2A & Sentinel-2B) operating 180° out of phase, highlighting how this configuration secures a 5-day revisit interval critical for tropical LULC mapping.
2. **Sensor Mechanics**: Replaced the whiskbroom scanning discussion with the Sentinel-2 **push-broom scanning mechanism** (linear array of CCD detectors), explaining its longer dwell time and subsequent improvement in signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). 
<truncated 1253 bytes>

IMAGE PREPROCESSING
Cloud and Shadow Masking.
Cloud contamination represents one of the most persistent challenges in optical remote sensing, particularly in tropical and sub-tropical environments where convective cloud formation is frequent throughout the year. To mitigate this challenge, scenes with minimal cloud cover (less than 10 percent) were preferentially selected during the data acquisition stage from the USGS EarthExplorer portal. The downloaded Landsat scenes were visually inspected within ArcGIS Pro to confirm that the study area extents were substantially free of cloud and cloud shadow contaminations.
Temporal Composition.

turns.content: Let me re-read the current version carefully, then do a proper academic rewrite from scratch.

Now let me do a proper academic rewrite. The current version has several weaknesses: it unnecessarily references Landsat/USGS as comparisons (this is a Sentinel-2 study, not a comparative one), the table splits Sentinel-2A and 2B unnecessarily (they are the same sensor), and some sections read as superficial substitutions rather than grounded Sentinel-2 methodology.
Now let me regenerate the Word document:

The original `.docx` is open in Word on your machine, so the updated version was saved as:

**`c:\YERBO\Desktop\Courage\precision agriculture\SENTINEL2_METHODOLOGY_REVISED.docx`**

Close the old `.docx` if you want to overwrite it directly next time.

### What changed in this rewrite

| Problem in the old version | How it was fixed |
|:---|:---|
| **Superficial Landsat-to-Sentinel substitution** — phrases like "rather than relying on manual scene-by-scene selection from the USGS EarthExplorer portal" and "Legacy issues associated with Landsat 7's SLC failure" had no place in a Sentinel-2 methodology | Removed all Landsat comparisons. Each section now stands on its own as a self-contained Sentinel-2 description |
| **Summary table split across 2A and 2B** — the sensors are identical; splitting them added no information and looked like padding | Consolidated into a single-column specification table |
<truncated 1275 bytes>
